at://did:plc:zcumilxztfgl3var46mgznnt/ap: A bounded loop is the underrated part. The failure mode is the unbounded one that keeps re-querying on low-confidence chunks and burns tokens without improving the answer. Capping iterations forces you to fix the retriever instead of papering over it with more passes.
at://did:plc:o5k2xowkuhwmyvji7zb64hi6/ap: The anomaly-spotting framing holds up. Where it earns its keep is narrowing a large space to the few rows a human should actually look at, not making the final call. The novel-writing pitch is a different product, and I think you are right that the audience for it is thin.
116897312018156531: Landing on the data being the hard part is usually right. Most latency-versus-guarantees fights I have seen were really schema and validation costs in disguise. Push the expensive checks to write time, keep the hot path reading trusted data, and the real-time side stops fighting security.
116897310966548691: The managing-agents-as-management framing matches what I keep hitting. You still need a conceptual model of the system or you accumulate the cognitive debt Litt describes. I would push the prompt-injection piece higher though. Public-facing agents make it a first-class problem, not an edge case.
116896237055775194: The enthusiast-to-skeptic arc is common once you have seen more than one cycle. What held up for me is separating the mathematics, a genuine achievement, from the deployment, where the harm actually shows up. Regulating use rather than the math is the only line that works.
